frameworks.name: recurrent neural network (connectionist)
frameworks.one_line: RNN hidden-unit geometry compared with human neural representations during magnitude comparison

frameworks.name: neural state space / population geometry
frameworks.one_line: parallel context-specific number lines aligned across contexts in neural state space

frameworks.name: neural normalisation
frameworks.one_line: normalisation reframed as a mechanism that supports relational transfer across scales

frameworks.name: abstract relational generalisation
frameworks.one_line: transfer of 'more/less' concept across contexts at different physical scales

core_question: How do humans and recurrent neural networks represent magnitude so that relational concepts like 'more' or 'less' generalise across contexts on different physical scales?
key_assumptions: magnitude comparison can be modelled with a shared task in humans and RNNs
multivariate signals (brain and hidden units) expose representational geometry
manipulations: context with different physical magnitude scales
human brain signals vs RNN hidden-unit activity as model systems
key_findings: both humans and RNNs developed parallel 'number lines' per context
context-specific number state spaces were aligned across contexts
alignment explicitly facilitated generalisation of 'more' and 'less'
interpretations: neural normalisation has a previously overlooked role in supporting transfer of abstract relational knowledge
aligned state-space geometry is a shared solution to magnitude generalisation in biological and artificial systems
